reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: CKP/Tanítanék documented commitment to equity of access and talent-identification across SES boundaries (Kockás könyv 2016; Tanítanék javaslatcsomag 2026); opposition to sorting mechanisms that exclude high-ability low-SES pupils before assessment.
reactions.interest: We defend the interest of high-ability low-SES pupils in fair access to selective places, and the public-good interest in talent identification across all social strata.
reactions.public_good_frame: A school system that screens for ability without screening by parental income or school choice power serves both efficiency (talent use) and equity (opportunity). Rebalancing who enters selective tracks — without eliminating them — preserves choice-driven selection for those who need it while reducing inherited advantage as a de facto admission criterion.
reactions.argument: S1 directly targets the documented high-SES skew in gimn entry (evidence: strong) without dismantling structures that serve pupils who benefit from early specialisation. The redesigned exam addresses coachability (a mechanism by which parental resources convert to test advantage), and regional quotas operationalise the principle that selective places should go to ability, not to whose parents can afford intensive prep. However, three load-bearing assumptions must hold: (1) a workable SES indicator must exist at application age — the scenario concedes this is 'unverified for Hungary'; (2) church/foundation maintainers must accept uniform quotas — consent terms are unknown; (3) the redesigned exam must actually reduce coachability — this is 'weak' evidence. The scenario explicitly flags that informal sorting may reappear if the exam is gamed differently. We support this direction IF these three conditions are met, but cannot endorse it as a solution unless they are.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support becomes unconditional if: (a) a feasibility study confirms a workable, non-gameable SES indicator can be built from Hungarian administrative data; (b) maintainer representatives confirm willingness to accept equity quotas under agreement renegotiation or subsidy conditionality; (c) pilot evidence demonstrates the redesigned instrument genuinely reduces preparation sensitivity. Opposition would follow if: (a) the SES indicator turns out to be a proxy that misfires systematically (e.g., address-based proxies that miss rural poor or urban poor in mixed postcodes); (b) church maintainers refuse quotas and demand exemption, fracturing the system; (c) informal sorting visibly replaces formal sorting, leaving the advantage structure intact.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: CKP/Tanítanék documented support for later selection age as a lever to reduce early tracking's inequality-amplifying effect (implicit in Kockás könyv framing of selection-timing as structural inequality driver); combined with commitment to avoiding middle-class flight that concentrates disadvantage in residual general schools.
reactions.interest: We defend the interest of lower-SES pupils in not being sorted out early, and the system-wide public-good interest in maintaining inclusive peer composition in lower-secondary education as long as possible.
reactions.public_good_frame: Moving selection later reduces the window in which family background and school choice can amplify initial ability differences into permanent track assignment. Later selection also permits better-informed choice and reduces misallocation of talent. However, this is only equitable if displaced demand does not migrate to non-state selective places, because if it does, the residual general schools become more socio-economically skewed while the selective places remain equally skewed — the compression of places to fewer families benefits the most mobile.
reactions.argument: S2 aligns with the timing-sensitivity literature (Chmielewski 2014; Brunello & Checchi 2007, both cited) on which CKP/Tanítanék bases its case for later selection. The cap is the mechanical lever: fewer early places = more cohort remains unsorted longer. However, the scenario identifies three critical risks: (1) advanced-programme funding in general schools 'is intended to' retain ambitious families but this link is 'weak' evidence — middle-class flight risk is 'moderate'; (2) the scenario concedes that without S1-type admission reform, residual selective places become 'mechanically more SES-skewed' — this is not a side-effect but a direct consequence of capping demand unchanged; (3) if displaced demand migrates to non-state places, 'transition-cohort harm is likely' because better-informed higher-SES families capture shrinking remaining state places first. We support S2 IF implemented together with S1 (simultaneous cap and admission reform) AND credible general-school quality guarantees (not mere funding, but pedagogical and staffing credibility). S2 alone, without S1, risks entrenching inequality within the disadvantaged group by offering scarcer selective seats to those families with the most school-choice knowledge.
reactions.condition_to_change: Conditional support becomes support if: (a) S2 is explicitly bundled with S1-type admission reform (quotas on the capped places) so the cap does not simply concentrate advantages; (b) general-school advanced-programme funding is backed by credible teacher-supply commitments, not formula-only promises; (c) simultaneous parallel caps are negotiated with church/foundation maintainers (the scenario names this 'assumption' but the budget shows it is not yet in place). Conditional support becomes opposition if: (a) S2 is enacted alone without S1, leaving shrinking selective seats to be allocated under existing high-SES-skew rules; (b) a successor government reverses the trajectory (Polish precedent cited) with no entrenchment lock in place; (c) longitudinal tracking shows migration to non-state selective places is substantial enough to leave residual general schools more skewed than they are now.

reactions.label: documented
reactions.source: Kockás könyv (2016) — CKP/Tanítanék foundational position arguing that tracking to age 10/12 is the structural driver of Hungary's high SES-outcome gradient; delayed selection is presented as necessary for equity. Tanítanék javaslatcsomag (2026) — comprehensive school to age 14 is the primary structural reform proposed.
reactions.interest: We defend the interest of all lower-SES pupils in not being sorted into lower-track schools early; we defend the system-wide interest in a genuinely common lower-secondary stage that keeps high-achievers and low-SES pupils in shared peer composition longer.
reactions.public_good_frame: A common lower-secondary stage to age 14 is the most direct structural lever to reduce the effect of family background on track assignment. This is not a compensation strategy; it removes the mechanism that embeds SES into school structure. Keeping high-achievers in common schools to age 14 creates a peer environment in which lower-SES high-ability pupils are identified later and on better information, and in which the asymmetric peer effects documented in the international literature can function (if they do).
reactions.argument: S3 is CKP/Tanítanék's documented structural position. The evidence base for delayed tracking → lower SES-gradient is moderate and cross-nationally consistent (Pekkarinen et al. 2009 Finland; Jakubowski et al. 2016 Poland; Hanushek & Woessmann 2006). However, the scenario itself identifies the load-bearing assumption: 'if sorting migrates to non-state schools rather than being eliminated, the equity case collapses.' This is not a flaw in S3; it is the identification of the real political-implementation risk. The scenario also flags three other critical uncertainties: (1) teacher supply — Hungary has documented shortage, contradicting the assumption that 'teacher supply can staff comprehensive lower-secondary'; (2) whether the asymmetric-peer-effect channel actually functions in Hungary — the evidence is 'contested' and Hungarian-specific evidence is 'weak'; (3) whether the SES gradient is caused by selection or merely reinforced by it — 'confidence: medium'. We support S3 structurally because it removes the sorting mechanism itself, not because it relies on compensation. But we note that implementation requires: (a) a pre-legislation multi-party pact (the scenario itself proposes this as the primary political defense); (b) explicit fallback to S2 if the pact fails (so no unanchored mandate); (c) piloting in 2-3 districts before national scaling; (d) an honest assessment of whether non-state migration can be controlled (the scenario names this as the equity case's make-or-break condition). The teacher-shortage assumption is the implementation bottleneck, not a reason to oppose S3 on equity grounds, because shortage is a resourcing question, not a structural question.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support is unconditional on equity grounds but conditional on implementation credibility. It remains support if: (a) a multi-party pact is negotiated and holds through the transition (reducing reversal risk to the level of S2); (b) piloting occurs and informs scaling rather than national rollout proceeding in parallel with pilot; (c) teacher-supply bottleneck is addressed via international recruitment, salary premia, or parallel specialist-teacher roles in common schools (not resolved, but honestly resourced). Opposition would follow only if: (a) the legislation is attempted without a pact (unanchored mandate, high reversal risk); (b) non-state school migration proves systematic and uncontrolled, and no fallback enforcement mechanism exists; (c) evidence from other countries' implementations shows that sorting simply moves to school choice between general schools (within-comprehensive sorting), which S3 does not prevent.

reactions.label: documented
reactions.source: Kockás könyv (2016) — CKP/Tanítanék argues that compensation strategies (non-structural reforms targeting general schools) do not address the root structural driver of inequality, which is early selection itself; compensation 'masks rather than offsets' sorting effects. Implicit rejection of pure-compensation models as insufficient for Hungarian equity context.
reactions.interest: We defend the interest of lower-SES pupils in structural equity (not being sorted early), not in compensatory equity (better funding of lower tracks). We defend the public-good interest in keeping peer-composition integrated longer as a precondition for genuine opportunity equality.
reactions.public_good_frame: Compensation packages improve absolute outcomes in disadvantaged schools but leave the sorting structure intact. If lower-SES pupils remain concentrated in general schools from age 10/12 onwards, even with better funding, the peer-composition gap and the signalling effect of early sorting persist. Structural selection teaches both the sorted-in and sorted-out that their futures are determined early; compensation does not change that message, it only makes the lower track slightly less poor. Genuine opportunity equality requires that the sorting mechanism itself be altered.
reactions.argument: S4 proposes to keep 6/8-year tracking unchanged and offset its effects through targeted funding (TEIP-style), curriculum standards, and school clustering. This is the compensation strategy that CKP/Tanítanék's documented position rejects. The scenario itself provides the evidence for this rejection: (1) 'Whether compensation can meaningfully offset, not just mask, the effects of early sorting' is rated 'low confidence' and is 'directly contradicted by S4's own uncertainties, which state no country has demonstrated this at Hungary's selection age'; (2) the scenario explicitly concedes that 'the sorting mechanism itself is left intact'; (3) it identifies a dynamic equity cost: 'sustained compensation risks legitimising early selection permanently, so if compensation only masks rather than offsets sorting, the package could entrench the very inequality it targets.' These are not implementation details; they are structural impossibilities. Compensating a sorting system can improve aggregate outcomes in the lower track, but it cannot equalise opportunity because opportunity is not purely a function of school funding — it is also a function of peer composition, institutional status, and the signal that sorting sends about who belongs where. Moreover, S4 is uniquely vulnerable to fiscal reversal: 'compensation lines are the first cut in fiscal consolidation' — the scenario rates this risk 'moderate' — which means the entire package is transient. Finally, S4 is the only scenario that does not address the teacher-shortage constraint at all; it assumes teachers can deliver standards and differentiation in larger, more heterogeneous classes without additional supply, which contradicts the documented shortage.
reactions.condition_to_change: Opposition is structural, not conditional, because the concern is not about whether S4 could improve absolute outcomes in general schools (it probably can, modestly) but whether it addresses the equity question at all. Opposition would become conditional support only if: (a) evidence emerged that compensation at Hungary's selection age (10/12) actually offsets rather than masks sorting effects — the scenario itself says this has not been demonstrated anywhere, and we have no basis to expect Hungary to be the exception; (b) the compensation budget were protected by constitutional or supermajority-lock entrenchment, making reversal costly enough to be credible; (c) S4 were explicitly bundled with a commitment to move to S2 or S3 within a defined period (so compensation is a transition, not a permanent solution). None of these seem likely, which is why S4 is rejected in principle, not merely in practice.
